core_behavior.note: Model A describes general load handling, Model B specifies detailed stateless architecture with auto-scaling and async queues, and Model C describes generic resource addition/removal, reflecting different design assumptions introduced without requirement specification.

inputs.note: Model B introduces scaling signals, configuration parameters, and traffic patterns as inputs, while Models A and C limit inputs to user requests and data volume.

outputs.note: Model B adds scaling event logs, capacity reports, and auditable records as outputs, while Models A and C focus on response consistency and monitoring metrics.

assumptions.note: Model B introduces a specific baseline of 1,000 concurrent users and a 10x target, while Models A and C make general architectural assumptions without numeric baselines, reflecting the requirement's failure to specify load targets.

failure_modes.note: Model A specifies graceful degradation and error messages, Model B specifies only alert issuance, and Model C describes unresponsiveness and component failure impacts without specifying recovery behavior.

edge_cases.note: Model B has an empty edge_cases array while Models A and C populate it with spike handling, resource unavailability, and non-uniform demand scenarios.

security_constraints.note: Model B has an empty security_constraints array while Models A and C specify data integrity, access control scaling, and compliance requirements.

performance_constraints.note: Model A specifies a 200ms response time threshold, Model C specifies 500ms, and Model B does not specify a latency threshold, representing incompatible numeric values for the same constraint dimension.

summary: ASM (Assumption Divergence) appeared most frequently across the eight fields, occurring in core_behavior, inputs, and assumptions.
